title: Top-Lock Backdoor
game_context: 4-on-4 half-court shell. Slot-to-slot ball reversal threatens, but your defender top-locks you above the wing — chest above the line, foot in front of you, blocking the up-pass to the wing.
possession_setup: You are the right wing. The ball is at the right slot. Your defender (x2) cheats above your shoulder to top-lock the up-pass back out to you.
decision_moment: x2 has hips opened toward the sideline, lead foot above your top foot, head turned to the ball.
visible_cue: x2 is above you, not between you and the rim. The lane between your spot and the basket is empty.
prompt: Your defender top-locked above you. The lane behind him is empty.

choices.label: Backdoor cut to the front of the rim.
choices.feedback_text: Best read. He's above you to take away the up-pass. The basket is wide open behind him — cut to the rim.

choices.label: Drift to the corner for a deeper catch.
choices.feedback_text: Okay. Drifting opens a corner pass, but you walked away from a clean layup the top-lock just gave you.
choices.partial_feedback_text: Drifting keeps spacing, but the better read was the empty rim behind the top-lock.

choices.label: Fight up the lane and ask for the ball above him.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. He's above you for a reason. Fighting up walks into his chest and the top-lock wins.

choices.label: Curl across the top to the other slot.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Cutting across the top lets x2 trail you and stays in front of x1's help. The lane behind him is the open one.

best_read: Plant the top foot, drop the shoulder, and back-cut behind x2 to the front of the rim. The passer leads you to the layup before x4 can rotate from the corner.
acceptable_reads: Drift to the corner — keeps possession, but you traded a layup for a longer catch.
bad_reads: Fight up to the wing — the top-lock wins; deflection or no-pass.
Curl across the top — x2 trails the curl and the cut runs into x1.
common_miss_reason: Players treat top-lock the same as side-deny and try to fight to the assigned spot. The cue (defender above the line) flips: behind him is open, not above him.
why_best_read_works: A top-lock means the defender is selling out to take the up-pass. He gives up the rim by definition — his chest is on the wrong side of the cut path. A backdoor punishes the very stance that took away the catch.
decoder_teaching_point: When a defender plays above you, the basket is behind him. Cut behind him, not around him.

feedback.correct: Good read. He played above you to kill the up-pass. The rim was open and you took it.
feedback.partial: Drifting kept the play alive. Next time, look at where his chest is. If he's above you, the rim behind him is open.
feedback.wrong: Watch his chest. If your defender is above the line of the ball, the basket is behind him. Cut there.

self_review_checklist: Did I see his chest above my top foot?
Did I plant and cut behind him, not around him?
Did I cut hard enough to beat the help?
Did I show my hands at the rim?

coach_validation.notes: Top-lock backdoor is universally taught at every level. Cue (defender above the line) is system-independent; cluster fits beginner cap (3 pre / 3 post).

source_research_basis: Universally taught youth-level read; top-lock backdoor is system-independent.
explanation_md: **The Backdoor Window — top-lock variant.** When a defender plays above the line of the ball, his chest is between you and the pass — not between you and the basket. Plant your top foot, drop your shoulder, and cut behind him to the rim. Show your hands. Watch his chest, not your spot.

wrongDemos.caption: You drifted to the corner. The pass came, but the layup was gone.
You fought up the lane. The top-lock won.
You curled across. x2 trailed the curl, x1 tagged it.
